steps.title: Sketch the bench first (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch the bench — two horizontal rails (seat and back) and four legs. The bench is the anchor; both figures will sit on it, so getting it placed and leveled first prevents the figures from floating.

steps.title: Place two gesture axes (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch an L-shaped gesture for each figure — torso vertical, thighs horizontal on the seat. Place them side by side. Notice the gap or closeness between them; that space already tells the story of who they are to each other.

steps.title: Block in heads and torsos (pencil)
steps.description: Add a small oval for each head. One figure may lean slightly toward the other — if so, let that lean show in the torso line. A 5-degree lean says more than you'd think.

steps.title: Rough in the lower bodies (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch both sets of legs — thighs resting on the seat, lower legs dropping down. If one figure is turned slightly toward the other, angle that figure's knees inward. Small adjustments in the legs describe the whole emotional relationship.

steps.title: Add the arms (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch each figure's arms. One arm might rest between them on the seat; one might be around the other's shoulder. Each arm needs one clear gesture — resist sketching more than two strokes per arm or it gets tangled.

steps.title: Check the proportions (pencil)
steps.description: Compare the two heads — do they sit at similar heights above the seat? Do the bodies feel the same scale? If one looks larger, erase and redraw that torso. Two figures in the same frame are always compared against each other.

steps.title: Switch to ink and ink the bench and back figure
steps.description: Switch to ink. Ink the bench rails and legs first, then the figure that reads as slightly farther from the viewer. Stop any of that figure's lines where the nearer figure will overlap it.

steps.title: Ink the front figure (ink)
steps.description: Ink the nearer figure over the top — its lines go cleanly over the area where the back figure ends. Add a small detail or two: a hairline, a collar edge, the shape of hands in the lap. Then stop. Two figures means the scene needs editing, not rendering.
